--- Design Rate Limiter ---

Hello.
Hey guys.
Welcome to Concept and Coding.
And today we are going to do a very very important question designed rate limiter.
But before going further, first you tell me in the comment that in which company you have faced this
question.
Right.
So with that being said, let's move and uh, let's see what how to design a rate limiter.
So first let's understand the problem.
Why we have why we need rate limiter.
So everybody I think knows of this right DDoS attack.
Right.
So here in DDoS attack what happen is attacker sends unwanted request to a server.
And each server has a limited resources Ram and disk space.
So everything is limited.
So now what happens is attacker send lot of thousands and even lakhs of requests in seconds that all
of these resources are consumed and the server becomes down.
And when the server becomes down, what happened to the genuine user?
So when genuine user do a request, their request will get declined.
So that's what is known as DDoS attack.
And to prevent this we bring the rate limiter.
And that's what it makes it.
The most important interview question that because as a backend software engineer you have to work on
APIs.
So you should be aware of how to, uh, limit a rate on APIs.
Okay.
So let's jump into the rate limiter part.
But before that before we do designing of this rate limiter.
Right.
There are certain algorithms which you should be aware of it.
Right.
Uh, without those it is very difficult to uh, come up with the design itself.
So let's there are five algorithms which you should know, right?
I will try to explain in a very simple and quick manner.
So first is token bucket, second is leaking bucket.
Third is fixed window.
Fourth is sliding window.
And fifth is.
Sliding window counter.
And this counter words come from fixed window counter, sliding window lock and sliding window counter.
So these are the five algorithm token bucket leaking bucket fixed window counter sliding window lock
and sliding window counter.
Right.
So let's start with first algorithm.
Token bucket right.
What does token bucket is.
So here understand with simple diagram.
So bucket as the name suggests there is a bucket.
So this bucket has certain property like capacity.
What is the capacity of this bucket to hold.
Capacity of what to hold token.
Let's say I say four.
So it can hold.
Let's say four.
Token.
Maximum.
Right.
If you try to add more token, it will.
It will overflow.
If you try to add more token okay.
And there is also something called refiller.
This is known as refiller.
So Refiller has a property that uh, that in 1 minute or 2 minute or three minute, how many token it
will add?
Let's say two token.
So this is all driven by configuration.
This is driven by config.
This is driven by config file.
Like we can change it when I say config file means these are dynamic values.
You can change it.
So token bucket has bucket which has capacity.
What it can hold tokens.
How much.
Let's say for example in this case four right.
And there is something called refill or refill.
Our work is to keep add tokens after a particular time.
In this case I'm taking that after one minute two tokens will be added.
If tokens are accesses means already four are there and one minute done.
So Refiller will add two tokens so there is no space in the capacity.
So those two tokens will get into overflow means they are removed.
So now how this token bucket is used.
So if you see that whenever a request are coming.
So whenever my particular request are coming, what it will do is it will first check.
So there will be first condition.
Right.
And the condition will be on the bucket.
Right.
What?
It will check.
Is there a token present?
Right.
If there is a token present, yes.
Then this request will consume one token.
This request will consume one token.
Let's say it has three token currently at present for example.
So when this request comes in, it consume one token and the count will reduce to two.
If there is no token, if the token is zero, then if there is a token has token no then it will request
will be denied.
Right?
So this is generally a small uh you can say that flow diagram how the token bucket works in.
So now you got this flow.
So now we can configure this token bucket like per user, per API wise.
Each user.
This is my rule for each user.
Three counter or three token.
You can say that three token right?
And for per minute, in per minute or in one minute right for post API.
Tweet post API let's say.
So this is one rule.
This is one rule.
So rule can be right for each user.

--- Load Balancer and Different Algorithms ---

Hey guys.
Welcome to Concept and Coding.
And this is Shreyansh.
So today we are going to cover this topic Load balancer.
So I am making this topic okay.
So now if you see not much topics are left in the theory part of the playlist.
We have covered so many topics and we are going to soon start the questions right.
So let's start with the load balancers and our different algorithm.
So first thing first what is load balancer.
Right.
So you know that we have a client server.
So our this is our client.
There can be many clients right.
There can be many clients.
And we have a server.
There can also be a many server.
Server one server two server N.
Right.
So when a request comes from the client, when a request comes from the client so that all the requests
should not goes to one server.
So one server should not get overloaded.
So what we do is we put load balancer.
The main purpose.
So now nowadays if you see the load balancer can do lot of things.
It can do logging, it can do so many other stuff.
But the main purpose of load balancer is to distribute this traffic appropriately to all the servers
so that no, no one single server get overburdened.
Now, if you see that the categories of load balancer there are two types.
L4 balancer, which is nothing but a network load balancer type L7 balancer which is nothing but a application
load balancer.
So you know that there are seven layers of OSI model.
So the seventh layer is your application.
And I think the fourth layer is your transport layer right.
So application layer is known as L7 balancer.
Network load balancer is known as your L4 balancer, which works at the transport layer.
Now what is the difference.
So here the major difference between the application layer load balancer and transport or network layer
load balancer is it can read your header session even data right.
It can also read your response also.
Right.
So what it can do is it can take decision to distribute this traffic based upon information from header
session cookies data.
Right.
And it can also have the capability to do the caching.
So it can also do caching also.
Why.
Because it can read the response right.
So you can say that it is much more advanced.
So this is much more Advanced right and in the other layer.
Network load balancer or L4 load balancer.
It can have your TCP port UDP port IP address.
IP address of source and destination.
So based on this only it can take a decision whether to route this traffic to which server.
Right.
So this is much more advanced but this is much more faster.
Now one more thing I just want to say before we start, there are a lot of algorithms you find over
the internet, right.
Like this, this, this.
They do not categorize it, whether it's a network or application, but based on my understanding of
the network and application load balancer, all these algorithms which are mentioned everywhere, everywhere,
like these are the load balancer algorithm.
This based on my understanding this comes under the network load balancer type.
So now let's first start with our algorithms which is first static algorithms.
Static load balancing.
The first static load balancing is your round robin.
So see everybody knows about the round robin right.
So this is your client right.
This is your client which is giving a request 1234.
It goes to the load balancer.
Now load balancer knows about this server okay.
So there are two servers.
Server one server two.
What it will do is in a round robin fashion.
So it goes first.
Now the second goes here.
Third goes here fourth goes here.
Now if there is more request comes fifth goes here, six goes here, seven eight.
And likewise.
So you know right round robin first server one.
Second.
Server two, third again server one, server to server one.
Server one two.
So this is a very simple round robin algorithm.
But there are certain advantage and disadvantage of it which is very very important to know about it.
Without this it would be incomplete.
So advantage of round robin is very very easy to implement right.

Asset property first is atomicity.

This got success.
Now credit B ₹200 to B.

rolled back okay.

So it has phase one and phase two okay.
So in the phase one generally known as the growing phase.
And phase two is known as the shrinking phase.
So like this understand that with this diagram that what is growing face and what is shrinking face?
So in the phase one, in the growing phase, what transaction can do is transaction can acquire the
locks.
Right.
So transaction can only acquire the locks.
The amount of number of locks with the time can only increase in the growing phase.
It cannot release in the growing phase.
It can only acquire more and more locks to perform a transaction after a time.
After a time, it move into the second phase, which is known as shrinking phase.
In the shrinking phase, now only transaction can release the locks it cannot acquire.
Now no more new locks, only it can release.
That's why it is two phase.
Phase one is the transaction request for the lock by the lock manager.
Okay.
So transaction can only request for the new locks.
The lock manager either Grant or denied.
The lock denied in case if lock is already held by some other transaction.

transaction cannot acquire any new locks.
Transaction is only allowed to release the lock.
Only allowed to release the lock which is taken previously.

--- Symmetric & Asymmetric Encryption with Explanation of AES, Diffie-Hellman ---

Hey guys.
Welcome to Concept and Coding.
And this is Shreyansh.
And today we are going to do a most important topic that is cryptography.
Why it is most important topic.
Because we are using it day to day.
We are using chat application which says that we are end to end encryption.
How you might be visiting website daily.
So many websites that are https secure, how you might be using JWT in your day to day work, office
work, what, how, how JWT do the authentication and integrity right?
So everywhere, no matter if you go one level deeper, you will find that cryptography got involved.
So that's why I said it's the most important topic because it make your foundation of system design
solid.
Okay, so without wasting any time, let's jump into the topic and let's try to understand it better.
What is encryption?
Encryption is the process of converting readable data into the text, which is difficult to understand,
which is also known as Cypher.
Right.
So a text which is easily readable form.
So through encryption we can convert it to a cypher okay.
Or the text which is difficult to understand.
So it makes use of cryptographic key right.
So consider this as an example.
Here is a very readable text concept coding okay.
Now I am passing it through a encryption method.
This encryption method make use of this key which is known as cryptographic key.
So encryption method make use of this key and perform its algorithm and convert this readable text into
a unreadable text or cipher okay.
So this encryption method might have its own algorithm for this purpose.
Let's say depending upon the key, its algorithm is that move two letters forward.
So C convert to F, right.
So c d e f right.
So key is two.
So skip two letters and then convert it to f.
So this is just a simple very very basic encryption algorithm okay.
So what I'm trying to say is that this encryption method uses key.
And based on its algorithm it do the encryption.
Similarly decryption method it accepts this unreadable text or cipher text.
Pass it to a decryption method along with the key.
So this key can be same or can be different depending upon the encryption algorithm which we are using.
But the thing is, this description method uses this cipher text plus key, which is also known as a
cryptographic key.
And then convert this back to a readable format.
So encryption is of two types.
Symmetric encryption a symmetric encryption.
And before I start I tell you that there is nothing like asymmetric encryption is only one which is
very highly used, because I know that there is a conception that symmetric symmetric encryption is
nowhere used everywhere.
Asymmetric encryption is used.
No, they are both like a left and right hand.
They are both used simultaneously.
I'll tell you how.
And they both are very popular.
Let's see that.
And I think by the end of this session you will get clarified why symmetric encryption and asymmetric
encryption both are equally important.
Not like asymmetric encryption is popular.
So symmetric encryption The same key is used for both encryption and decryption.
So we have seen an example at above right.
So let's say you have a user this is sender.
This is receiver right.
So sender is sending the data passing to an encryption method.
And let's say key used is five.
And after this it got data.
Cipher data.
This is plaintext.
This is cipher text right.
And in the decryption now this receiver what it will do is it pass this cipher text to a decryption
method.
And it uses the same key.
Key equals to five.
Only it uses the same key.
And get the again plaintext.
So symmetric encryption is same.
Key is used for both encryption and decryption.
Asymmetric encryption is that uses two different keys that is private and public key.
So here again you have a user.
So it has a plain text.
Okay.
So here it might call an encryption method using key one right.
And it gets a cipher text.
And once this receiver got the cipher text it passed it to a decrypt method.
And the key it might use is that some key two but different key.
What I'm trying to say that different key right.
And then they get the plain text again okay.
So symmetric encryption same key is used.
Or symmetric encryption two different key is used which is known as private key and public key depending
upon the uh like algorithm.
Let's say uh, you are what operations you are performing.
Let's say you are encrypting the data then encryption can be done through public key and decryption
can be happen through private key.
But there are certain scenarios like digital signature encryption happens to private and decryption
happen through public.
So you can say that this what key to use for encryption decryption depending upon the scenario.
We will see that okay.
But first part is clear right.
What is the difference between these two.
Then the second part is that what are the important algorithm for symmetric.
So symmetric.
There are two algorithms DS and DS.
DS is popular earlier.
But now it is not recommended because I think in 2005 uh, it has been uh, cracked.
Right.
Why it is cracked.
Because the key size length is only 56 bits.

And once you get the hash value, you can match it.
What the plain data hash and what the signature hash you got.
Is it equal or not?
If it is equal means data is not changed at all.
If it is not equal means this plain data has been modified in mid.
Right.
So this digital signatures this is how it helps authentication and integrity of data identification.
Right.
And that's why it is also known as asymmetric encryption because it uses private key and public key.
So during signing it is signed through private key of a sender.
And during verifying it uses the public key of the sender only.
Okay.
I hope you got little bit understanding of cryptography.
Okay, guys, uh, if you have any doubt, please bring me in the comment section.
Uh, we can discuss more.
Thank you.

--- API GATEWAY and Microservices Architecture ---

Hey guys, and welcome to Concept Encoding.
And today in System Design Playlist I am going to cover a new topic which is very, very important,
which is API gateway.
There are so many questions which build on top of it.
And I'm going to tell you the complete, uh, overall design where this API gateway fit and these two
are the most frequently interview questions get asked.
If API gateway is a single entry point, how does it handle millions of requests per second or sometimes?
Also like how API gateway is different from load Balancer?
Does it come before or after the load balancer?
And trust me, so many engineers has doubt on this one.
What is API gateway?
In simple terms, it accepts the client API request and route them to correct backend service based
on API endpoint.
Read this line again, because this is what makes it different from a load balancer.
So let's say that this is a client and API gateway is in mid, so it accepts your request.
Now API gateway will decide hey if it is this API slash API slash invoice it has to route to this microservice
invoice microservice.
If API structure is like this it has to route to order microservice.
If API structure is like this, it has to route to sales microservice.
Okay.
So it routes the API to correct backend service.
Isn't this what load balancer also do?
No.
Generally load balancer simply distribute the traffic to multiple instance of a microservices.
So for example once the request is coming load balancer task is to just equally distribute the traffic
between multiple instances of a same microservice.
So invoice microservice one, invoice microservice two, invoice microservice three, and many more.
But it do not have the capability to understand an API.
Okay.
And then take a decision where to route.
So load balancer doesn't have capability to understand this API okay.
It is slash invoice.
I have to move it to invoice.
Otherwise I have to move it to order.
No.
And that's the major difference between API gateway and load balancer.
So apart from routing what API gateway is doing right.
Based on the end point, it decide where to route which microservice to route.
It has so many other capabilities, right?
API gateway is very intelligent compared to load balancer.
So first thing is API composition.
So here if you see I will tell you the problem with the API composition.
So now let's say that you are opening a e-commerce website and you are going to like view my order,
view my order, click on button.
Now you have to see your past purchase history.
Now let's say if you are clicking that option from Mobile device.
So mobile device has very less bandwidth.
You can say that.
So we show less details.

So mobile device Client is using an API.
Just take it as an example one from the product microservice and one from the invoice microservice.
And fetching the detail of product and fetching the detail of invoice and showing the details.
But now let's say if you are using computer or a personal computer there, it has more space and bandwidth.
So you might show more details.
So maybe the same page for the PC device client.
Apart from product and invoice, it is also showing ratings and reviews.
It is might be also showing recommendations.
So even though the same page but based on the device also you see the functionality differs.
Okay, now on the PC device line you need to query more APIs.
Or maybe it is possible that different microservices also to fetch those details.
Now here if you see this is an additional headache on the client.
So with the help of API composition feature of API gateway this can be simplified.
So now here if you see that in the API gateway we can configure the endpoint.
Let's say slash API slash my order.
If the client calls this now API gateway has a Intelligent that okay, if it is a mobile device, I
will call this two API.
Those two microservices and fetch that product detail and invoice detail API gateway has an intelligent
okay.
If it is a personal computer device then I have to call additional APIs.
Okay.
Gather the result and return the single response.
So now here, if you see that the client work is and the complexity is reduced a lot.
So this is this feature is known as API composition.
And it is very very important.
And it is very frequently used in Netflix.
So Netflix the API gateway which uses it is highly used this API composition.
Okay.
Then we have another feature of API gateways authentication.
So what happen is so let's say you have a client they are calling an API gateway.
Okay, now API gateway also has capability to authenticate the client.
So now let's say that if it is using OAuth 2.0 flow.

Hey the client.

to 9 too many requests.

What is the maximum concurrent request API gateway can handle?
Then we have API throttling.
API throttling.

Very important.
So now understand that you have multiple components.

There are multiple instances of everyone.

Who does that service discovery.
A microservices can scale up and scale down.
It's necessary to know the location, IP address and port.
Service discovery keep track of those.
So there are two different approaches.
Approach one.

Another approach is that service discovery.
Keep the health check of all the registered microservice and keep only active microservices location.
So earlier it has multiple microservices which is registered in the service discovery.

--- How DNS works? | System Design of Domain Name System ---

Hey guys, and welcome to Concept Encoding.
And today in part we are going to cover the Domain Name System.
It's sometime looks very easy.
But at the same end if you go in depth it becomes a little complicated also.
So let us start without wasting any time.
So the first thing very basic is what is IP address.
IP address is a unique numerical label assigned to each device connected to internet.
So consider them as an address so that any other device can locate them over the internet.
So you already know IPv4 and IP v6.
So these are two kind of addresses which is assigned to any device which is connected over the internet.
What is domain name.
So domain name.
It's a human readable and friendly address used to identify a device on the internet.
So one point we are saying that device has a unique numerical label, let's say 170 2.3, 2.4.8 something.
So it's very difficult.
It's not human readable and it's not easy to remember if there are multiple devices you have to communicate
with.
So the more easy and readable, friendly way for human is assigning a name to them that is known as
domain name, like google.com, amazon.com, then what is DNS?
DNS is nothing, but you can say a mediator, which helps to translate the domain name into IP addresses
because over the internet device only understand this IP address.
Nobody understands this amazon.com google.com.
So some system is required which translate this name to IP address.
And that's where DNS comes into the picture.
So this is all okay, right?
One liner is good, but how?
How actually it does this thing that is more important.
So currently there are two approach recursive and iterative.
I will tell you both.
But before even going to the first part which is recursive, let's see this one.
So whenever let's say you are typing W-w-w-what concept encoding.com.

And uh, there is no such way to determine how much how many microservices.
So I will tell you like how to answer this question.
But let me first tell you what why we want Microservices like, because ultimately, when we are going
to divide the monolithic system into multiple services, like what is our expectation from each services?
Like if those expectations are fulfilled, then only we can say that yes, expectation is fulfilled.
We can consider the one microservices, right?
So why we want microservices?
What is the expectation out of it?
First one would be it should be loosely coupled.
Loosely coupled means if you are updating microservices one.
If you are some doing an update on here, there is no need to update this also.
So they are independent to each other.
The second thing would be like engineers can independently code, test and deploy.
What does it mean is like now you have two microservices.
Microservices.
Microservices two.
They have their own set of engineers.
And it's evolving.
Microservices are evolving independently, right.
So it's deployment is nowhere dependent on this.
It's testing is nowhere dependent on this and vice versa.
Okay.
The third achievement we want to get by dividing into microservices is uh you can say that less.
Communication overhead.
Less communication overhead is means that there should not be any scenario that for each operation or
each small thing, you have to communicate between these two services.
Right.
So more communication overhead means more latency, more complexity, more dependency, which ultimately
breaks your loose coupling.
Okay.
So less communication overhead.
And another which I would say that like, uh, scale independently.
So now again two microservices.
Now let's say this microservice has more traffic than this.
So it can scale independently.
Here I have 100 it's instances.
But this microservice is uh, traffic is not that much.
Okay.
I can have only it's, uh, ten micro, ten, ten instances.
So they should scale independently.

So if you have to do that means somewhere there is tight dependency means if more traffic is coming
here then ultimately this is also calling this much to this so that its traffic is also increasing means
there is some very tight coupling somewhere.
Okay.
So it they should scale independently.
So these are the four things which we have to keep in mind like what we expect from microservices.

--- Attacks with Demo | CSRF, XSS, CORS and SQL Injection ---

Hey guys, and welcome to Concept and Coding.
And you already know that in Spring Boot I'm going to start Spring Security.
And to understand it better it's very much essential to understand the common attacks also.
Then only we would be able to understand the importance of spring security.
Okay, so in today's video I will cover very, very important topics with demo like how these attacks
happen and how we can protect them.
Like we can see that also in in detail spring security, how to protect these attacks.
But today let's understand what are these attacks, how it happened with a demo.
The first very important.
And sometimes it's asked in an interview also CSRF cross-site request forgery.
What does it do.
Just see this point.
First user is already authenticated to a site.
Okay let's say that you have one side something.
Let's say Dot com.
You are already authenticated.
Okay, the CSRF attack tricks a browser into making unwanted requests to a site where user is already
authenticated.
So what does it line says that.
So this means that this attack, what it does is that your browser.
So now let's say that for a minute.
This is a session based.
You can say that authentication your browser okay.
Your browser in your cookie has a session stored has a session ID is stored.
Now let's say that when you get some link click here.
And this is link is hosted by some attacker.
Like it's not from a genuine and you somehow attacker tricked you that hey click you click here, then

C okay.

it publish an event.
Okay.

an event even.

system.

Like Kafka we need to make sure that both are inconsistent state.

But while persisting the data into DB got failed.

But your DB it got failed.
So ultimately the whole request is failed.

So this would become a very, very good project.
Also let's start with transactional outbox pattern okay, so transactional outbox pattern in this pattern,
instead of writing in both DB and publishing an event to message broker.
What it will do it first write the event into a separate table called outbox outbox table.
Generally, a new table is created which is known as some let's say outbox outbox table in that one
event is first write into that.
Okay, so now since we are writing into a DB table itself, whatever you are the original main right,
you have to write into DB plus this event.
Both can be in the same transaction.
Okay.
So say this one I have a user service let's say and user service want to create user okay.
So I begin the transaction I insert a user.

user event outbox table let's say.
So I created one event table.
Okay so let me call it a user event table where I am publishing only user events okay.
There could be a two scenarios where you have a common outbox table where no matter user order invoice,
all events goes into this only or you have different different table for like user events order events.
So you publish into that only.
So depend upon your business uh choice.

But for here now let's say user service.
First it created its main task.
It's inserted a user into the DB into the user table, and then it inserted the user event into this
one outbox table okay.
And then commit the transaction.
So now you see that this is one transaction.
One transaction means if any failure happens, the complete rollback will happen.
Now this is one transaction.
So at least now we are sure that the main user insertion is happened.
Plus its event is also there in the DB.
If it is a success, if transaction failure both will not have the record.
Okay.
Now once it is done there we have to write a new poller service.

--- Service Discovery Introduction ---

Hey guys, and welcome to Concept Encoding.
And today in microservices topic I am going to cover a very important part is service discovery.
Okay.
So in microservices service or component are created and deleted dynamically.
We can know that right.
So let's say our instance is itself.
Let's say we have our microservices order.
Definitely in microservices one of the advantage is that we can have as many instances based on the
traffic, but these number keep on changing.
If traffic is more, I can spawn more instances.
If traffic is less, I can reduce the number of instances.
So instances are created and deleted dynamically.
We cannot hard code the URL of a particular instance.
It's not scalable and not feasible.
So till now while we seen Rest template Rest client fan client order service is making call to product,
service and everywhere we have hard coded the URL, so it's okay for the testing perspective.
But in production we never hard code these URLs, right?
So in above example, order service has hard coded the URL of the product service.
And it is okay till we have only one instance of product service.
If you have only one instance then it's okay.
You can hard code it.
But in case if you have multiple product service instance, right.
And which is very very possible.
Right.
Let's say in this scenario.
So I have instance one, instance two instance three all have different your IP address and port number
all has different IP address and port number.
But if you have hard coded the URL your traffic will always goes to one of the service only always goes
to one of the service only.
Okay.
And it is also possible that a particular stance can be removed, but since you have hard coded it,
you will try to connect to an instance which is removed.
Then you will face connectivity issue, right?
So there are a lot of challenges like first is single point of failure.
Single point of failure is is hard coded instance of product service goes down order service will not
be able to communicate with other instance.
That's what I told.
Like if you have hardcoded it and I have removed this instance, then you won't be able to communicate
it.
So it's a single point of failure.
No load balancing.
So even though I have three services, since you have a hard coded it every time the traffic goes to
this instance only.
Even though my these two other instances are sitting idle okay.
Tight coupling.
So because of hard coded URL in the order service, there is a tight coupling between order service
and product service.
Now let's say that I have to move the product service okay.
And maybe it's IP and port address need to be changed.
Then you first have to update the order service and then only you can migrate your product service.
Otherwise it's not possible.
Okay.
So it brings the tight coupling difficulty in testing different environment might use different URL
which require frequent changes in your config.
So if you have hardcoded it let's say now you are testing on r dev.
Then you need to change this.
If you're testing on stage you need to change this.
Okay.
So with different test environment a production QA dev different URL.
And then you have to keep on changing it if you are doing a hard coded.
So one of the way we can overcome all this limitation is our service discovery.

Then we have token bucket.
Token bucket is very, very simplified versions.
And uh, what happened is we have a kind assume it like we have a bucket and we place the limited tokens
inside it.
Each request consume one token if there is no token left, request is denied and there is also a refiller
which adds the token in bucket at regular time interval.
If bucket capacity is full and cannot hold more token, token overflow will happen and token will be
rejected.
So understand with this is that this is one bucket which has capacity of four token.
So 12344 tokens are present.
There is a refiller which task is to add one token every six seconds.

Come.
Any token left.
No.
So it is rejected.
Now at sixth second, a refiller adds one token, So Refiller added one token into the bucket because
we have configured it.
Add one token every six seconds.
So at six seconds one token is added.
Now if any request comes, it will use it and accept it.
Very very simple.
So disadvantage is that a quick burst is possible if token capacity is not set properly.
What if I have put a token that in a bucket I have capacity equals to 1000 I.
Without doing proper computation, I give random capacity then means that much token can get accumulated.
And what if in a one second it's a sudden request comes all 1000, so all 1000 will get accepted.
So there is a chance of burst traffic.
Okay.
Because whatever the amount of tokens present, that much concurrent request can be accepted.
So you have to consider this token capacity properly.
Okay.
Another is leaky bucket.
Leaky bucket is considered as like requests are queued and accepted at fixed rate.
If queue is full, incoming request will overflow.
So let's say you have an incoming request.
Okay bucket.
Or you can say that a queue which is keep on accumulating the request.
Okay.
And the queues are accepted at a very constant or very fixed rate.
So consider this with the flow diagram a request comes if queue is full.
If this queue is full then it will send denied and it will send http 4 to 9.
Too many requests can't even accept.
If queue has some space then it will add the request at the end of the queue and from the queue Our
system will keep accept the request in a very constant rate.
Very, very constant rate.
So very simple.
But disadvantage is your latency will increase because requests might have to wait in queue for its
turn.
So request which is present here.
It has to wait for the previous request to finish and to be accepted.
And your queue is become a bottleneck.
So site size should be set properly.
If the size of the queue is very small, you would be denying lot many requests.
If the size is too big, your latency will increase a lot.

--- Bulkhead Pattern Introduction ---

Very quick rate limiter.
It protects our application from clients.
This is why I am telling you again about rate rate limiter in just one minute is because clear distinction
need to be present between rate limiter and bulkhead, because many times a confusion happens like which
one is bulkhead?
Which one is rate limiter.
So focus here is that rate limiter protects our application.
Let's say our application is order.
It protects our application.
From whom?
From clients.
So let's say this is my client.
And client is sending me too many requests.
So it limited limit them.
Hey how many requests we can accept within a specific window right.
And also one more thing is rate limit and never talks about concurrency.
So just rate limiter.
Rate limiter says that hey I can accept ten requests in one minute.
Either you can send me ten requests in one shot or one by one.
It never talks about concurrency.
It just says that.
What is the limit?
Okay, but it protects us from our clients.
Now let's go to the bulkhead that it helps to control how many concurrent requests can go to downstream
service.
Now let's say this is our service order.
Now I am more concerned about the request which I am sending to my downstream.
Now, I am not concerned about the request which I am getting from my clients.
Okay, so that is one of the major difference.
And bulkhead majorly talks about the concurrent request.
We will see that.
And if you understand this two use cases right.
So there are two possible use cases.
If you understand those two use cases, no matter when an interviewer or in your implementation like
a scenario is put, you would be able to clearly understand that whether rate limiter or bulkhead need
to be applied.
So understand that one is very very simple use case.
Let's say you have order.
This is your component and this is your downstream product.
So order invokes product API.
Let's say product is a small and lightweight service and can only handle three concurrent requests okay.

--- Retry Pattern : Fault Tolerance in Distributed Microservices ---

Before I start, I just want to say that that retry, if in not properly implemented, it has more disadvantages
than advantages.
We will get to know why.
So in distributed system, call to downstream service might fail because of transient issues.
Transient issues means that short temporary issue like network issue, timeout, etc. and retry of same
request after a short delay can get succeed.
Okay, so one question might comes to you that hey still why we should do retry?
Why can't user or client can invoke our API again.
So let's say that this is our client.
This is our component order.
And we have an API.
And we are invoking another API of let's say product component.
And there is some transient issue while invoking it.
So what we are saying is so transient issues.
If we retry it, it can get succeed.
So we can do a retry for this invocation.
So the question is that hey why we need to retry.
Why can't client again invoke the API.
So that automatically it will invoke the product API again.
Why we have to retry ourself.
So see the answer is for each call lot of heavy processing is done and it's not free of cost, memory,
time, resources, etc. everything like it's there is some cost incurred.
So say you have invoked client at first invoke an API.
90% of the API implementation is completed.
Task is completed.
Only 10% is left.
And then it invoke this product API and the transient issue happen.
Let's say, uh, network timeout happen.
So is it a good idea to dispose of this 90% of the task which is done?
No.
Right.
We if we can just simply retry this, and if it gets succeeded, then only 10% tasks have to be done.
Otherwise, if we ask client to retry again, our API hit again, our API again we will do the same
90% task again.
So redundant of the work is there, duplicate of the work is there and it will increase our cost and
it's not free of cost to be honest, right?
So with above understanding we know that retry is important, right?
I think I hope this point will tell you that why retry is important?
Because we don't want to do the same 90% task again.
Okay.
And I'm just saying this is just one client.
If there are hundred clients For this API and they are all invoking and there are thousands of calls.
So you are saying that you are doing a lot of wasting your, uh, capacity on just duplicate tasks which
you have already completed.
So we know that retry is important, but the question is when to retry.
How to retry.
Very, very important question.
When to retry.
First of all is do not retry on permanent failures.
Permanent failures is like no matter how many times you will retry, it will always fail.
Like for errors.
So for errors generally are in the category of validation error.
Validation error.
Validation error means the input request input request which client has passed has is not correct.
Until unless client will not change, the input validation error will not remove.
So if you retry with the same request previously, again you will see a validation error.
So generally these are known as permanent failure for xx.
Do not retry if operation is non patent.
What does it mean.
So hydro patency makes sure that if request is retried multiple times duplicate processing will not
happen.
So say you have a service A which invokes the create API of service B okay.
So it has invoked it.
And while service B writes into its DB but it takes certain time okay.
And here it says that timeout happen and timeout happens.
So for service A the request has been timeout.
But service B internally inserted the row into the DB.
Now since this is a timeout and user has retried.
So this is a retried again.
Now here service B be?
Insert the row again.
Id2.
Earlier it was already inserted.
Id1.
Now it is Id2.
So here.
This is not my intention.
My intention was not to create a duplicate row for the same request.
Okay, so we can say that this this API, the API of the service be this create API is not idempotent.
Okay.
So that's why we say that do not retry if operation is not idempotent.
Otherwise you will end up in creating duplicate resources.
So if you do not know how to design idempotent APIs, I have already covered it into the high level
design playlist.

Like what are the challenges, how we can come up with the API design?
So have a look in case you have a doubt.

What five x error says that it is system error.

So first is fixed interval retry.
So what does fixed interval retry says that retry after a constant delay.
So within each retry the delay is constant.
So say that the attempt total attempt is four.
So when we say total attempt means original one call plus three retries.
So total four.
Okay.
So after original call there has to be a fixed delay.
Like let's say my fixed delay is two second.
So it would be like after two second first retry will happen.
Then it will wait for two second then second retry will happen.
Then it will wait for two second then third retry will happen.
So there is a fixed interval of delay within the retries.
So let's say this is the original call which happened at two.
So first retry happen at four, then second retry will six.
Third retry at eight.
So each one has two two seconds gaps here.
So advantage of this is it is very very simple easy to configure and debug also.
But this is main disadvantage is that higher chance of retry storm which is also known as thundering
herd problem.
Thundering herd problem means many clients retry at the same time, causing a surge of retry traffic
and system spend resources on retries instead of handling new requests.
So understand with this is that.
Say that you have a load balancer and you have, let's say component service a service a multiple instances
instance one, instance two, instance three, and load balancer distribute the traffic between the
instances of service.
A there are so many clients client one, client N and the traffic is also huge.
Now let's say that a buggy code has been introduced into the service a and it is.
Start throwing some exception.
And if client start doing retry also since already it's a heavy traffic and for that they are all started
doing retry at a fixed delay.
So all are retrying.
So original request.
Let's say there are 1000 request original.
Now after two second delay.
Retry one will happen.
Okay.
So everyone will do retry 1000 requests again after two second delay.
Retry two.
They again do a retry okay.
So these are just retry traffic whatever the normal traffic new request is coming.
That is different.
So means your system is now get bombarded with the retry traffic.
And it is possible that while you are only servicing retry traffic, you somehow decline the new request
which is coming.
That is also possible.

it is one of the frequently asked interview question.

But yeah, this is one of the disadvantage is that if all the clients retry at the same interval, it
is possible that the retry traffic become huge and it can cause thundering herd problem.
So fixed interval is clear.

--- Circuit Breaker Introduction ---

What does Circuit Breaker says?
This pattern prevents an application to make repeated calls to downstream service that is likely to
fail.
Okay, so we'll come to this line again.
But see this diagram.
So let's say our application is order service right.
And we and this product is a downstream.
So order is invoking product for.
Now assume that this product service is unavailable.
So no matter how many times you call, this call will fail.
Right.
So product service is down no matter how many times whether you are doing a retry of the same call or
whether you are making a totally different call, it will fail.
So there is some disadvantages for it.
The first disadvantage is we put unnecessarily add load to the product service and because of that product
service might take longer time to recover.
So see product services already unavailable, right?
It is facing some issue and we are adding more load to it.
So there is a chance that product service might take little longer time to, uh, come up in the healthy
state.
The second is order services unnecessarily wasting its resources, both latency and the thread blocking.
So just see that if, let's say there are thousands and thousands of calls and product service is unavailable,
they all will fail.
So order service is unnecessarily wasting its resource because there is a latency involved.
It calls it.
It waits for the product service to response till timeout, and then only it will send to the client
that hey, it's a fail some five x error something.
Okay.
So there is definitely increase in latency and you are unnecessarily blocking your thread on this call.
Okay, so there is no need because even if you waited, the call is likely going to fail because the
product service is unavailable.
But now what is the solution?
How does order Service know when to stop the downstream call?
Like we are saying that hey order don't make unnecessarily call to product because it is unavailable,
right?
But how?
Order knows that when it is unavailable, when it is up and when I okay if it is unavailable, when
again I have to restart.
So something has to instruct this.
Uh, you can say that knowledge.
Somebody has to put this knowledge to order, right.
Then only order would be able to stop or restart the call.
So who at that knowledge, that is circuit breaker.
Okay.
So again, revisiting the line which we went ahead in the first place, it prevents an application to
make repeated calls to downstream service that is likely to fail.
So see this diagram now.
So now order service let's say make initial calls.
And both got failed.
So now we will put some threshold value that hey if this many calls get failed means there is some issue
with the product.
So don't make any further calls to it.
So now after that, once that threshold limit is reached, we will stop the future call to product.
So now all future calls.
There is no hit to the product.
We will fail the call immediately.
We will not block the thread.
We will fail the call immediately.
Okay.
And we will wait for some time.
After that we will check if product service is healthy or not, so it will do a trial testing.
After some time, hey, it will send some sample.
Or you can say that the test calls hey is product service is responding properly or not?
If yes, then only it will allow future calls to the product.
Okay, so whatever we have covered here it is can be understand.
It can be understand through this state diagram.
So start from here.
This is the initial state okay.
So for any application the best case is closed.
Closed means that all calls are allowed to downstream service means everything is going good okay.
Circuit is closed.
Means just remember always sometime many times engineer get confused that hey, circuit is closed.
means no calls.
No.
So see, consider this as a circuit.
So when the circuit is closed, then only electricity will flow.
When the circuit is open, then electricity current will break.
Understand?
It is just to remember this.
So when the circuit is closed, all calls are allowed to downstream service.
Now if failure limit cross the threshold let's say five failure six failure.
Whatever the threshold we will set if that many failures.
So see there could be some success.
There could be some failure while making a call to downstream.
But if the failure limit threshold value is crossed, then it moved from closed state to open state.
Now, open state means no calls allowed to downstream service and fail the call immediately.
So after this it wait for a certain time, which is like you can say that timeout period.
After that, let's say it could be ten second, five second, 15 second that, hey, for five second
or ten second, no call is allowed.
After that it moved to half open state.
Half open state says that allow only limited number of test calls to downstream and track their success
rate.
Say I say that hey allowed only three calls.
Allowed only five calls.
So very limited number of calls.
We will send it to the downstream and then track their success rate.
If the success rate is 100%, then circuit can be closed again.
But if the success rate is not 100%.
The state goes back to open.
Okay, so whatever we have seen this can be easily represented through this state of circuit breaker.
